title: Capital Budgeting Process
description: Understand the steps and considerations in capital investment decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital investments are the largest financial decisions organizations make. CMAs analyze investments, build business cases, and monitor project performance. These decisions shape the company's future for years or decades!

sections.title: What is Capital Budgeting?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
The process of planning and evaluating significant investments in long-term assets.

**Characteristics:**
• Large initial outlay
• Benefits over multiple years
• Often irreversible
• Significant strategic impact

**Examples:**
• New plant or facility
• Major equipment purchases
• Acquisitions
• R&D investments
• IT systems

sections.title: Project Classifications
sections.content:
**By purpose:**
• **Replacement:** Maintain operations or reduce costs
• **Expansion:** Increase capacity for existing products
• **New products/markets:** Strategic growth
• **Regulatory/safety:** Mandatory compliance

**By relationship:**
• **Independent:** Can accept/reject separately
• **Mutually exclusive:** Accept one means reject others
• **Contingent:** One depends on another

**Classification affects how we evaluate!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capital Budgeting
sections.content:
**"IESIM"** - The five-step process:

**I**dentify opportunities
**E**valuate alternatives
**S**elect best projects
**I**mplement chosen projects
**M**onitor results (post-audit)

**CMAs are involved in every step!**

sections.title: Relevant Cash Flows
sections.content:
**Include these cash flows:**
• Initial investment (negative)
• Operating cash inflows
• Working capital changes
• Terminal/salvage value
• Tax effects

**Exclude:**
• Sunk costs (already spent)
• Allocated overhead (not incremental)
• Financing costs (in discount rate)

**Key principle:** Only INCREMENTAL cash flows matter!

sections.title: Post-Implementation Audit
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
• Compare actual vs. projected results
• Identify reasons for variances
• Improve future forecasting
• Hold managers accountable

**Challenges:**
• Isolating project's specific contribution
• Changed assumptions over time
• Reluctance to admit poor decisions

**Best practice:** Compare to original approval document, not revised forecasts!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital budgeting evaluates large, long-term investments
Five steps: Identify, Evaluate, Select, Implement, Monitor
Projects classified by purpose and relationship to others
Only incremental cash flows are relevant
Exclude sunk costs and allocated overhead
Post-audit compares actual to projected returns
CMAs build models and support decisions throughout process

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical characteristic of capital budgeting decisions?
options: A small initial outlay relative to the company's total assets.
Benefits expected to be received over multiple years.
Potential strategic impact on the organization.
Often involves investments that are difficult to reverse.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Capital budgeting decisions involve significant investments in long-term assets. A small initial outlay would not typically be considered a capital budgeting decision.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the scale of projects usually considered for capital budgeting.

reveal.question: Define an 'Independent' project in the context of capital budgeting.
reveal.answer: An independent project is one whose acceptance or rejection does not affect the acceptance or rejection of other projects. The decision to undertake the project is made solely on its own merits.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is considering upgrading its existing safety equipment to comply with new government regulations. How would this project most likely be classified?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Projects undertaken to comply with laws or regulations are classified as Regulatory/Safety projects. These are often mandatory and may not directly contribute to profitability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the primary driver behind the project.

rows.label: Definition
values: Acceptance/rejection of one project does not affect others.
Acceptance of one project automatically rejects others.

rows.label: Decision Making
values: Can accept multiple projects if each meets the investment criteria.
Must choose only one project, even if multiple meet the investment criteria.

rows.label: Example
values: Investing in new equipment for two separate, unrelated product lines.
Choosing between two different locations for a single new factory.

title: Net Present Value (NPV)
description: Calculate and interpret NPV for investment evaluation

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: NPV is the gold standard of capital budgeting! It directly measures value creation in today's dollars. CMAs must master NPV calculations and understand why NPV is theoretically superior to other methods. Exam heavily tests this!

sections.title: NPV Formula
sections.content:
**Net Present Value:**

NPV = Σ [CFt / (1 + r)^t] - Initial Investment

**Where:**
• CFt = Cash flow in period t
• r = Required rate of return (discount rate)
• t = Time period

**Decision rule:**
• NPV > 0 → Accept (creates value)
• NPV < 0 → Reject (destroys value)
• NPV = 0 → Indifferent (earns exactly required return)

sections.title: NPV Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Project data:**
• Initial investment: $100,000
• Annual cash flows: $30,000 for 5 years
• Required return: 10%

**Present Value of annuity:**
PVA = $30,000 × PVIFA(10%, 5)
PVA = $30,000 × 3.7908 = $113,724

**NPV:**
NPV = $113,724 - $100,000 = **$13,724**

**Decision:** Accept! Project creates $13,724 of value.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: NPV Interpretation
sections.content:
**"NPV = How Much Richer"**

Positive NPV tells you exactly how much wealthier the shareholders become in TODAY'S dollars.

• NPV of $50,000 = Shareholders are $50,000 richer
• NPV of -$20,000 = Would destroy $20,000 of value

**That's why it's the best measure!**

sections.title: Why NPV is Superior
rows: Assumes reinvestment at cost of capital (realistic)
Sum of project NPVs = Portfolio NPV

sections.title: Choosing the Discount Rate
sections.content:
**Options for discount rate:**

**Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC):**
• Most common for average-risk projects
• Reflects company's overall cost of capital

**Risk-adjusted rate:**
• Higher rate for riskier projects
• Lower rate for safer projects

**Hurdle rate:**
• Minimum acceptable return
• May include strategic premium

**Wrong rate = Wrong decision!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: NPV vs. IRR Conflict
sections.content:
**When NPV and IRR give different rankings:**

This happens with mutually exclusive projects of different:
• Size (scale)
• Timing of cash flows
• Project life

**Always choose based on NPV!**

NPV measures actual value creation; IRR can mislead when projects differ significantly.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NPV = PV of inflows - Initial investment
Accept if NPV > 0 (creates shareholder value)
NPV shows value creation in today's dollars
Use WACC or risk-adjusted rate for discounting
NPV is theoretically superior to other methods
When NPV and IRR conflict, use NPV
NPVs are additive across projects

knowledgeCheck.question: Project X has an initial investment of $200,000 and is expected to generate annual cash inflows of $60,000 for the next 5 years. The company's required rate of return is 12%. Using the present value of an annuity factor (PVIFA) of 3.6048, what is the best course of action?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the present value of the annuity: $60,000 * 3.6048 = $216,288. Then, calculate the NPV: $216,288 - $200,000 = $16,288. Since the NPV is greater than 0, the project should be accepted.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the present value of the cash flows before determining the NPV.

reveal.question: Write out the Net Present Value (NPV) formula and define each component.
reveal.answer:
NPV = Σ [CFt / (1 + r)^t] - Initial Investment

Where:
CFt = Cash flow in period t
r = Required rate of return (discount rate)
t = Time period

knowledgeCheck.question: How does an increase in the required rate of return (discount rate) generally affect the Net Present Value (NPV) of a project, assuming all other factors remain constant?
options: Has no effect on the NPV
The effect is unpredictable and depends on the project's cash flows
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An increase in the discount rate means that future cash flows are worth less in today's dollars. Therefore, a higher discount rate reduces the present value of the future cash flows, leading to a lower NPV. If the discount rate is high enough, the NPV may become negative.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the denominator in the NPV formula.

title: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
description: Calculate and evaluate investments using IRR

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: IRR is popular because it's expressed as a percentage, making it easy to compare to cost of capital. CMAs must understand IRR calculations, its limitations, and when it can mislead. Many executives prefer IRR, so you need to interpret it correctly!

sections.title: IRR Definition
sections.content:
**Internal Rate of Return:**
The discount rate that makes NPV = 0

Solve for r where:
Σ [CFt / (1 + r)^t] = Initial Investment

**Decision rule:**
• IRR > Required return → Accept
• IRR < Required return → Reject
• IRR = Required return → Indifferent

**IRR represents the project's percentage return on investment.**

sections.title: IRR Calculation
sections.content:
**Trial and error or financial calculator/Excel:**

**Example:**
• Initial investment: $100,000
• Year 1 CF: $60,000
• Year 2 CF: $60,000

**Find r where:**
-$100,000 + $60,000/(1+r) + $60,000/(1+r)² = 0

**Solution:** IRR ≈ 13.1%

**At 10% required return:** Accept (13.1% > 10%)
**At 15% required return:** Reject (13.1% < 15%)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: IRR Meaning
sections.content:
**"IRR = Breakeven Cost of Capital"**

IRR tells you the maximum cost of capital at which the project is still acceptable.

• If IRR = 15%, you can borrow up to 15% and still break even
• If cost of capital > IRR, you're paying more than the project earns

**IRR is the project's "interest rate"**

sections.title: IRR Limitations
rows: All positive or all negative CFs

sections.title: Modified IRR (MIRR)
sections.content:
**MIRR addresses reinvestment problem:**

1. Discount all negative cash flows to present at financing rate
2. Compound all positive cash flows to terminal value at reinvestment rate
3. Find MIRR that equates PV of costs to FV of benefits

**Formula:**
MIRR = (Terminal Value / PV of Costs)^(1/n) - 1

**MIRR uses realistic reinvestment rate (usually WACC)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Non-Conventional Cash Flows
sections.content:
**When cash flows change signs multiple times:**

**Conventional:** - + + + + (one sign change)
• One IRR exists

**Non-conventional:** - + + + - + (multiple sign changes)
• Multiple IRRs possible!
• Each sign change can create another IRR

**Example:** Project with cleanup costs at end
-$100, +$200, +$200, -$150

**Solution:** Use NPV profile or MIRR

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IRR = Discount rate where NPV = 0
Accept if IRR > Required return
IRR is the project's percentage return
Limitation: Assumes reinvestment at IRR (unrealistic)
Multiple IRRs possible with non-conventional cash flows
Scale problem: High IRR on small investment may not be best
When conflicting with NPV, use NPV for decision

knowledgeCheck.question: Project X has an initial investment of $500,000 and is expected to generate annual cash flows of $150,000 for the next 5 years. The calculated IRR for Project X is 12%. If the company's required rate of return is 11%, what decision should be made regarding Project X?
options: More information is needed to make a decision
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since the IRR of 12% is greater than the required rate of return of 11%, the project is expected to generate a return higher than the minimum acceptable return. Therefore, the project should be accepted.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the relationship between IRR and the required rate of return.

reveal.question: Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR) in a single sentence.
reveal.answer: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate that makes the net present value (NPV) of all cash flows from a particular project equal to zero.

knowledgeCheck.question: A project has an initial investment of $200,000 and generates the following cash flows: Year 1: $80,000, Year 2: $70,000, Year 3: $60,000, and Year 4: $50,000. If the IRR is calculated to be 9%, and the company's cost of capital is 10%, what does this imply?
options: The project is profitable and should be accepted.
The project is unprofitable and should be rejected.
The project will break even at the 9% discount rate.
The project's NPV will be positive at the 10% cost of capital.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRR of 9% means that the project's NPV is zero when discounted at a 9% rate. Since the cost of capital is 10%, which is higher than the IRR, the project's NPV at 10% will be negative, making the project unprofitable at that cost of capital. However, it breaks even at 9%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Relate the IRR to the NPV at different discount rates.

title: Payback and Other Methods
description: Apply payback period, discounted payback, and accounting rate of return

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: While NPV is theoretically best, many companies use simpler methods too. Payback measures liquidity risk, and ARR connects to accounting returns. CMAs must understand all methods because different stakeholders prefer different measures!

sections.title: Simple Payback Period
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Time required to recover the initial investment.

**Formula (even cash flows):**
Payback = Initial Investment / Annual Cash Flow

**Decision rule:**
Accept if Payback < Maximum acceptable period

**Example:**
Investment: $100,000, Annual CF: $25,000
Payback = $100,000 / $25,000 = **4 years**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Payback Uses
sections.content:
**"PILL"** - When payback makes sense:

**P**reliminary screening
**I**lliquidity concerns
**L**imited time horizon
**L**ack of data for detailed analysis

**Use as supplement to NPV, not replacement!**

sections.title: Profitability Index (PI)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
PI = PV of Future Cash Flows / Initial Investment

OR: PI = 1 + (NPV / Initial Investment)

**Decision rule:**
• PI > 1 → Accept
• PI < 1 → Reject

**Example:**
PV of inflows: $113,724, Investment: $100,000
PI = $113,724 / $100,000 = **1.14**

**Useful when capital is rationed:** Rank by PI to maximize NPV per dollar invested.

sections.title: Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
ARR = Average Accounting Profit / Average Investment

OR: ARR = Average Net Income / [(Initial + Salvage) / 2]

**Example:**
Average net income: $15,000
Initial: $100,000, Salvage: $20,000
Average investment: ($100,000 + $20,000) / 2 = $60,000

ARR = $15,000 / $60,000 = **25%**

**Uses accounting income, not cash flows - major weakness!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Payback: Time to recover investment (ignores TVM)
Discounted payback: Uses PVs (still ignores post-payback)
PI = PV of inflows / Investment (useful for capital rationing)
ARR uses accounting income (not cash flows)
Payback useful for liquidity assessment and screening
All methods have limitations - NPV remains best
Use multiple methods for complete picture

knowledgeCheck.question: Project X requires an initial investment of $200,000 and is expected to generate the following cash flows: Year 1: $50,000, Year 2: $60,000, Year 3: $70,000, Year 4: $80,000. What is the payback period for Project X?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The cumulative cash flows are: Year 1: -$150,000, Year 2: -$90,000, Year 3: -$20,000.  Payback occurs between years 3 and 4. Payback = 3 + ($20,000/$80,000) = 3 + 0.25 = 2.71 years
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the cumulative cash flows for each year until the initial investment is recovered.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the payback period when cash flows are even?
reveal.answer: Payback Period = Initial Investment / Annual Cash Flow

sections.title: Payback Period vs. Discounted Payback Period

rows.label: Time Value of Money
values: Ignores the time value of money.
Considers the time value of money.

rows.label: Decision Making
values: Simpler and easier to calculate, but less accurate.
More complex to calculate, but provides a more accurate picture of profitability.

title: Risk Analysis in Capital Budgeting
description: Incorporate risk into capital investment analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cash flow projections are uncertain. CMAs must quantify this uncertainty through risk analysis! Single-point estimates can be dangerously misleading. Sensitivity and scenario analysis reveal what drives project risk and inform better decisions.

sections.title: Sources of Project Risk
sections.content:
**Revenue risk:**
• Price uncertainty
• Volume uncertainty
• Competition
• Economic conditions

**Cost risk:**
• Input cost volatility
• Labor costs
• Operating efficiency

**Execution risk:**
• Construction delays
• Cost overruns
• Technology problems

**Identify key risk drivers for each project!**

sections.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Change ONE variable at a time to see impact on NPV.

**Example:**
Base case NPV: $50,000

| Variable | -10% Change | +10% Change |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Price | -$30,000 | +$70,000 |
| Volume | -$20,000 | +$60,000 |
| Costs | +$60,000 | +$40,000 |

**Conclusion:** NPV most sensitive to PRICE changes.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sensitivity Analysis
sections.content:
**"One at a Time"**

Sensitivity analysis changes ONE variable while holding others constant.

**Shows:**
• Which variables matter most
• Breakeven points for each variable
• Where to focus risk management

**Limitation:** Ignores correlations between variables!

sections.title: Scenario Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Change MULTIPLE variables together for different scenarios.

**Common scenarios:**
• Base case (most likely)
• Best case (optimistic)
• Worst case (pessimistic)

**Example:**
| Scenario | Price | Volume | Costs | NPV |
|----------|-------|--------|-------|-----|
| Best | $55 | 12,000 | $30 | $150,000 |
| Base | $50 | 10,000 | $35 | $50,000 |
| Worst | $45 | 8,000 | $40 | -$30,000 |

**Better than sensitivity: Considers correlations!**

sections.title: Monte Carlo Simulation
sections.content:
**Most sophisticated approach:**

1. Assign probability distributions to key variables
2. Computer randomly samples from distributions
3. Calculate NPV for each sample
4. Repeat thousands of times
5. Analyze distribution of outcomes

**Results show:**
• Expected NPV
• Standard deviation of NPV
• Probability NPV < 0
• Range of possible outcomes

**Requires specialized software (Excel @RISK, Crystal Ball)**

sections.title: Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate
sections.content:
**Increase discount rate for riskier projects:**

**Company WACC:** 10%

| Project Type | Risk Adjustment | Rate Used |
|--------------|-----------------|------------|
| Replacement | -2% | 8% |
| Expansion | 0% | 10% |
| New product | +3% | 13% |
| New market | +5% | 15% |

**Higher rate → Lower NPV → Harder to accept**

**Compensates for risk in the cash flows themselves!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Certainty Equivalents
sections.content:
**Alternative to risk-adjusted rate:**

• Convert risky cash flows to "certainty equivalents"
• Discount at risk-free rate
• CE = Risky CF × Certainty Coefficient

**Example:**
Risky CF: $100,000, Certainty coefficient: 0.85
CE = $100,000 × 0.85 = $85,000
Discount $85,000 at risk-free rate

**Both methods should give same answer if done correctly!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sensitivity: Change one variable, see NPV impact
Scenario: Change multiple variables together
Simulation: Probability distributions and thousands of trials
Risk-adjusted rate: Higher rate for riskier projects
Sensitivity reveals key drivers; Scenario addresses correlations
Understand probability of negative NPV, not just expected NPV
Match risk analysis method to project importance

knowledgeCheck.question: A project has a base case NPV of $100,000. Sensitivity analysis reveals the following: a 5% decrease in sales price decreases NPV by $40,000, a 5% increase in variable costs decreases NPV by $20,000, and a 5% increase in fixed costs decreases NPV by $10,000. Which variable is the MOST sensitive to changes in the project's NPV?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sales price is the most sensitive because a 5% change results in the largest change in NPV ($40,000). While costs also impact NPV, the impact of a price change is greater.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the absolute value of the NPV change for each variable.

reveal.question: Name the three primary categories of project risk discussed in this lesson.
reveal.answer: The three categories are: Revenue risk, Cost risk, and Execution risk.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Assess project viability under different conditions

knowledgeCheck.question: A project's profitability is heavily dependent on securing a specific patent. Failure to obtain this patent would significantly reduce projected revenues. This risk is best categorized as:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: This is Revenue Risk because the uncertainty surrounding obtaining the patent directly impacts the project's potential revenue stream.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which category of risk is most directly affected by the patent outcome.

title: Real Options and Strategic Value
description: Evaluate managerial flexibility and strategic options in capital budgeting

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: NPV assumes passive management, but projects often include valuable flexibility! Real options capture the value of being able to expand, abandon, or delay. CMAs who understand real options can justify strategic investments that basic NPV might reject.

sections.title: What are Real Options?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
The right, but not obligation, to take future actions based on new information.

**Key insight:**
• Traditional NPV assumes fixed plan
• Real options recognize management flexibility
• Flexibility has value, especially under uncertainty

**Option value increases with:**
• Greater uncertainty
• Longer time to decide
• More flexibility

sections.title: Types of Real Options
rows: Right to increase scale if successful
Build plant with room to add capacity
Right to change inputs or outputs

sections.title: Option to Expand Example
sections.content:
**Base project:**
• Investment: $100,000
• Expected NPV: -$10,000
• Standard analysis: REJECT

**With expansion option:**
• If market conditions favorable (40% probability)
• Can invest additional $150,000
• Additional NPV: $80,000

**Value of option:**
40% × $80,000 = $32,000

**Adjusted NPV:** -$10,000 + $32,000 = **$22,000**

**Decision with option: ACCEPT!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Real Options
sections.content:
**"FADED"** - Types of real options:

**F**lexibility to switch
**A**bandon if failing
**D**elay investment
**E**xpand if successful
**D**ivide into stages

**Total value = NPV + Value of options**

sections.title: Option to Abandon
sections.content:
**Value of being able to exit:**

**Without abandon option:**
• Must continue even if project fails
• NPV includes full downside

**With abandon option:**
• Can sell assets/exit if failing
• Limits downside losses
• Salvage value sets floor

**Example:**
Project fails 30% of time, loss = $200,000
Abandonment recovers $150,000
Option value: 30% × ($200,000 - $50,000 loss) = $45,000 saved

sections.title: Strategic Investments
sections.content:
**Some investments create future opportunities:**

• R&D projects (platform for future products)
• Market entry (beachhead for expansion)
• Learning investments (develop capabilities)
• Standard-setting (influence industry)

**These may have negative NPV but positive strategic value!**

**CMA challenge:** Quantify strategic value without wishful thinking

**Approach:** Identify and value specific options created

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Real options = value of managerial flexibility
Common options: Expand, Abandon, Delay, Switch, Stage
Total value = Traditional NPV + Option value
Options more valuable with higher uncertainty
Strategic investments often contain embedded options
Abandonment option limits downside risk
Real options can justify investments that NPV alone rejects

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the key difference between traditional Net Present Value (NPV) analysis and the real options approach in project valuation?
options: Traditional NPV assumes fixed investment decisions, while real options recognize and value managerial flexibility to adapt to future conditions.
Traditional NPV incorporates all possible future scenarios, while real options only consider the most likely scenario.
Traditional NPV discounts cash flows at a higher rate than the real options approach.
Traditional NPV is used for short-term projects, while real options are used for long-term projects.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The core difference lies in the recognition of management's ability to react to new information. Traditional NPV assumes a static plan, whereas real options acknowledge the value of flexibility.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how management can change course after a project has started.

reveal.question: What is the MOST important factor to consider when evaluating an option to abandon a project?
reveal.answer: The salvage value of the project's assets, as this represents the potential recovery of investment if the project is terminated. This value should be compared against the present value of continuing the project under adverse conditions.

knowledgeCheck.question: According to real options theory, how does increased uncertainty (e.g., higher volatility in market conditions) generally affect the value of a real option?
options: Increased uncertainty decreases the value of the option.
Increased uncertainty increases the value of the option.
Uncertainty has no impact on the value of the option.
Increased uncertainty only affects the option value if the underlying asset has a negative expected return.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Greater uncertainty increases the potential for large positive payoffs while limiting downside losses (since the option not to proceed can be exercised). This asymmetric payoff profile makes the option more valuable.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the potential for both upside and downside scenarios.

title: Post-Audit of Capital Projects
description: Learn how to evaluate completed capital investments to improve future decision-making

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Capital investments often don't deliver promised returns! Post-audits hold managers accountable, identify estimation biases, and improve future investment decisions. CMAs play a key role in designing and conducting these reviews.

sections.title: What Is a Post-Audit?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A formal review of a completed capital project comparing actual results to original projections

**Timing:**
• 1-3 years after project completion
• When project reaches steady-state operations
• Sufficient time to measure actual performance

**Purpose:**
• Accountability - Did we deliver what we promised?
• Learning - What can we improve for future projects?
• Continuous improvement - Reduce estimation bias
• Feedback loop - Close the planning-to-results cycle

sections.title: Post-Audit Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: Gather Data**
• Original capital request and projections
• Actual costs incurred
• Actual revenues/savings achieved
• Timeline comparison (on time vs. delayed)

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Performance**
• Recompute NPV/IRR using actual cash flows
• Compare to original projections

**Step 3: Analyze Variances**
• Identify significant deviations
• Determine root causes
• Distinguish controllable from uncontrollable factors

**Step 4: Document Lessons**
• What went right? What went wrong?
• How can future estimates improve?

**Step 5: Report Results**
• Present findings to management
• Recommend process improvements

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Post-Audit Benefits
sections.content:
**"CLIP" - Why post-audits matter:**

**C**apture lessons for future projects
**L**imit overoptimism in proposals
**I**mprove estimation accuracy
**P**romote accountability

Without post-audits, managers learn nothing from past investments!

sections.title: Common Variance Categories
rows: Were estimates realistic? Was scope controlled?
Were milestones achievable? What caused delays?
Market changes, competitive response, adoption rate
Were forecasts reasonable? Did market change?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Post-Audit Challenges
sections.content:
**Why Post-Audits Are Difficult:**

**1. Sunk Cost Bias**
• Resistance to admitting bad decisions
• "We've already spent the money"

**2. Changed Circumstances**
• Hard to compare when market conditions shifted
• What was "actual" vs. projection?

**3. Measurement Problems**
• Separating project impact from other changes
• Identifying incremental cash flows

**4. Political Resistance**
• Sponsors may resist negative findings
• Need organizational support for honest reviews

sections.title: Best Practices
items: **Document original assumptions** clearly for later comparison
**Track patterns** across multiple projects for systematic bias
**Include post-audit requirements** in capital request policy

sections.title: Behavioral Impact
sections.content:
**Knowing a Post-Audit Will Occur:**

**Positive Effects:**
• More realistic projections (less "hockey stick" forecasts)
• Better upfront analysis
• Stronger supporting documentation
• Increased accountability

**Potential Negative Effects:**
• Risk aversion - managers avoid bold projects
• Gaming - structuring to pass audit criteria
• Blame avoidance - unwillingness to propose innovations

**Balance:** Make audits learning opportunities, not punitive exercises

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Post-audits compare actual project results to original projections
Conduct 1-3 years after project completion when results are measurable
Process: Gather data, calculate actual performance, analyze variances, document lessons
Common variances: cost overruns, delays, revenue shortfalls, operating cost increases
Post-audits promote accountability and reduce estimation bias
Challenges include sunk cost bias, changed circumstances, and political resistance
Focus on learning and improvement, not blame

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the PRIMARY purpose of conducting a post-audit of a capital investment project?
options: To assign blame for cost overruns
To improve the accuracy of future capital budgeting decisions
To determine whether the project should be abandoned
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The primary purpose of post-audits is to learn from completed projects and improve future capital budgeting decisions. This includes identifying estimation biases, understanding what went right or wrong, and promoting accountability for more realistic forecasting.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the forward-looking benefits of reviewing past decisions.

reveal.question: What factors make post-audits difficult to conduct effectively?
reveal.answer: Key challenges include: (1) Sunk cost bias and resistance to admitting bad decisions, (2) Changed circumstances that make comparison difficult, (3) Measurement problems in isolating project impact, (4) Political resistance from project sponsors, and (5) Difficulty separating controllable from uncontrollable variances.

title: Capital Rationing and Project Selection
description: Learn to select optimal projects when capital is limited and projects compete for funding

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Companies rarely have unlimited capital! When good projects exceed available funds, CMAs must help select the combination that maximizes shareholder value. This is where the Profitability Index becomes essential.

sections.title: What Is Capital Rationing?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Limiting capital investment below the level that would be optimal if funds were unlimited

**Types:**

**Hard Rationing:**
• External constraint (can't raise funds)
• Market conditions, credit restrictions
• Company at debt capacity

**Soft Rationing:**
• Internal, self-imposed limit
• Management policy decision
• Conservative growth strategy

**Key Implication:** Cannot accept all positive-NPV projects
Must choose projects that maximize value per dollar invested

sections.title: Profitability Index (PI)
sections.content:
**Formula:**

PI = PV of Future Cash Flows / Initial Investment

**Or equivalently:**
PI = (NPV + Initial Investment) / Initial Investment
PI = 1 + (NPV / Initial Investment)

**Decision Rules:**
• PI > 1: Accept (positive NPV)
• PI < 1: Reject (negative NPV)
• Higher PI = better value per dollar

**Example:**
Project A: NPV = $50,000, Investment = $200,000
PI = ($50,000 + $200,000) / $200,000 = 1.25

Project B: NPV = $30,000, Investment = $100,000
PI = ($30,000 + $100,000) / $100,000 = 1.30

**Project B has higher PI = more value per dollar invested!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: PI vs NPV
sections.content:
**When to use which:**

**NPV** = Best for UNLIMITED capital
• Accept all positive NPV projects
• Maximizes total wealth

**PI** = Best for RATIONED capital
• Rank by value per dollar
• Maximizes wealth per dollar invested

**Remember:**
NPV tells you total dollars created
PI tells you efficiency (bang per buck)

sections.title: Project Selection Under Rationing
sections.content:
**Process:**

**Step 1:** Calculate NPV and PI for all projects

**Step 2:** Rank projects by PI (highest first)

**Step 3:** Select projects in order until capital exhausted

**Example:**
Capital budget: $500,000

| Project | Investment | NPV | PI |
|---------|------------|-----|----|
| A | $200,000 | $60,000 | 1.30 |
| B | $150,000 | $52,500 | 1.35 |
| C | $250,000 | $50,000 | 1.20 |
| D | $100,000 | $25,000 | 1.25 |

**Ranked by PI:** B (1.35), A (1.30), D (1.25), C (1.20)

**Selection:** B + A + D = $450,000 invested
Total NPV = $137,500

**vs selecting C + A:** $450,000 invested
Total NPV = $110,000 (worse!)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: PI Limitations
sections.content:
**When PI Ranking Can Mislead:**

**1. Indivisible Projects**
Can't do 0.5 of a project - must consider combinations

**2. Multi-Period Rationing**
Capital constraints over multiple years complicate analysis

**3. Mutually Exclusive Projects**
PI may rank smaller project higher even if larger NPV available

**4. Scale Differences**
Small high-PI project may leave capital unused

**Best Practice:**
Use PI for initial ranking, then check combinations to maximize total NPV within budget

sections.title: Integer Programming Approach
sections.content:
**For Complex Situations:**

**When Simple Ranking Fails:**
• Projects have dependencies
• Multiple constraints (capital + labor)
• Multi-period rationing

**Integer Programming:**
• Decision variable: accept (1) or reject (0)
• Maximize: Total NPV
• Subject to: Budget constraint(s)

**Example Formulation:**
Maximize: 60,000X_A + 52,500X_B + 50,000X_C + 25,000X_D
Subject to: 200,000X_A + 150,000X_B + 250,000X_C + 100,000X_D ≤ 500,000
Where X = 0 or 1

**Practical Note:** Usually solved with software

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital rationing means choosing among positive-NPV projects
Hard rationing is external (market); soft rationing is internal (policy)
PI = PV of cash flows / Initial investment (or 1 + NPV/Investment)
PI > 1 means positive NPV; higher PI means more efficiency
Under rationing, rank by PI to maximize value per dollar
Check combinations when projects are indivisible
NPV still matters for total wealth creation

knowledgeCheck.question: Project X requires an initial investment of $400,000 and has a net present value of $80,000. What is the profitability index?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: PI = (NPV + Initial Investment) / Initial Investment = ($80,000 + $400,000) / $400,000 = $480,000 / $400,000 = 1.20. Alternatively, PI = 1 + (NPV/Investment) = 1 + ($80,000/$400,000) = 1 + 0.20 = 1.20.
knowledgeCheck.hint: PI equals the PV of cash flows divided by initial investment, or equivalently 1 + (NPV/Investment).

reveal.question: When might NPV and PI rankings give conflicting recommendations, and how should a manager resolve the conflict?
reveal.answer: Conflicts occur when: (1) Projects differ significantly in scale, (2) High-PI project has lower total NPV than low-PI project. Resolution: Under unlimited capital, choose higher NPV (total wealth). Under capital rationing, use PI to maximize wealth per dollar invested. If high-PI project leaves capital unused, check whether combinations of smaller projects can generate more total NPV.

title: Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR)
description: Overcome IRR limitations with a more realistic reinvestment assumption

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: IRR has a major flaw - it assumes intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR itself, which is often unrealistically high! MIRR corrects this by using a more realistic reinvestment rate, giving a more accurate picture of project returns.

sections.title: IRR's Reinvestment Problem
sections.content:
**The Hidden Assumption in IRR:**

When calculating IRR, the math implicitly assumes that all intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the IRR.

**Example:**
Project with IRR = 25%
Year 1 cash flow: $100,000

**IRR assumes:** This $100,000 earns 25% until project end

**Reality:** Company probably earns its cost of capital (say 10%) on excess cash

**Result:** IRR overstates the true project return

sections.title: MIRR Solution
sections.content:
**Modified IRR Uses Two Rates:**

**1. Finance Rate (for negative cash flows)**
• Cost of funding outflows
• Usually the cost of capital
• Discounts outflows to present

**2. Reinvestment Rate (for positive cash flows)**
• Rate earned on inflows
• Often cost of capital or a realistic return rate
• Compounds inflows to terminal value

**MIRR Formula:**
MIRR = ⁿ√(Terminal Value of Inflows / PV of Outflows) - 1

Where n = number of periods

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: MIRR Steps
sections.content:
**"PTM" - Three-Step Process:**

**P**resent value of all OUTFLOWS (negative CFs)
• Discount to time zero at finance rate

**T**erminal value of all INFLOWS (positive CFs)
• Compound to end of project at reinvestment rate

**M**IRR calculation
• Find rate that equates PV to TV
• MIRR = (TV/PV)^(1/n) - 1

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When to Use MIRR
sections.content:
**Use MIRR when:**
• IRR is unrealistically high
• Project has non-conventional cash flows
• Comparing projects of different sizes
• More realistic return estimate needed

**MIRR ≤ IRR always** (when reinvestment rate < IRR)

**Decision Rule:**
• MIRR > Cost of Capital → Accept
• MIRR < Cost of Capital → Reject

**Exam Note:** If given both finance and reinvestment rates, you're calculating MIRR!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IRR unrealistically assumes reinvestment at the IRR rate
MIRR uses a specified reinvestment rate (usually cost of capital)
MIRR solves the multiple IRR problem for non-conventional cash flows
Process: PV outflows, TV inflows, then find connecting rate
MIRR ≤ IRR when reinvestment rate is below IRR
MIRR is more consistent with NPV ranking
Accept if MIRR > cost of capital

knowledgeCheck.question: A project has an IRR of 28% and a cost of capital of 12%. If MIRR is calculated using 12% as both the finance rate and reinvestment rate, the MIRR will most likely be:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: MIRR will be less than IRR (28%) because it uses a lower reinvestment rate (12% instead of 28%). However, since the project was acceptable under IRR (28% > 12%), it will also be acceptable under MIRR (MIRR > 12% cost of capital).
knowledgeCheck.hint: MIRR is lower than IRR when the reinvestment rate is below the IRR.

title: Cost of Capital Estimation
description: Calculate cost of debt, cost of equity, and weighted average cost of capital

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cost of capital is the HURDLE RATE for investments! Every capital budgeting decision uses it as the minimum acceptable return. CMAs must understand how to calculate WACC and its components to evaluate projects and measure economic value added.

sections.title: Component Costs Overview
sections.content:
**WACC = Weighted Average Cost of Capital**

**Formula:**
WACC = (Weight of Debt × After-tax Cost of Debt) + (Weight of Equity × Cost of Equity)

**Components:**
• **Cost of Debt:** Interest rate on borrowing, adjusted for tax shield
• **Cost of Equity:** Return required by shareholders
• **Weights:** Based on target or market value capital structure

**Example:**
Debt: 40% of capital, 8% interest, 25% tax rate
Equity: 60% of capital, 12% required return

WACC = (0.40 × 8% × 0.75) + (0.60 × 12%)
WACC = 2.4% + 7.2% = 9.6%

sections.title: After-Tax Cost of Debt
sections.content:
**Interest is tax-deductible!**

**Formula:**
After-tax Cost of Debt = Pre-tax Rate × (1 - Tax Rate)

**Example:**
Bond yields 8%
Tax rate 25%

After-tax cost = 8% × (1 - 0.25) = 6%

**Why it matters:**
• Debt is "cheaper" than it appears
• Tax shield reduces effective cost
• $100 interest expense saves $25 in taxes
• Net cost is only $75

**Sources for pre-tax rate:**
• Yield to maturity on bonds
• Interest rate on bank loans

sections.title: Cost of Equity: CAPM
sections.content:
**Capital Asset Pricing Model:**

**Ke = Rf + β × (Rm - Rf)**

**Where:**
• Ke = Cost of equity
• Rf = Risk-free rate (Treasury rate)
• β = Beta (systematic risk)
• Rm = Expected market return
• (Rm - Rf) = Market risk premium

**Example:**
Risk-free rate: 3%
Market return: 10%
Beta: 1.2

Ke = 3% + 1.2 × (10% - 3%)
Ke = 3% + 1.2 × 7%
Ke = 3% + 8.4% = 11.4%

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Beta Interpretation
sections.content:
**Beta measures volatility relative to market:**

**β = 1.0:** Same volatility as market
**β > 1.0:** More volatile (riskier, higher return required)
**β < 1.0:** Less volatile (safer, lower return required)

**Examples:**
• β = 1.5: Stock moves 50% MORE than market
• β = 0.8: Stock moves 20% LESS than market

**Higher risk = Higher required return!**

sections.title: Alternative: Dividend Growth Model
sections.content:
**Gordon Growth Model:**

**Ke = (D₁ / P₀) + g**

**Where:**
• D₁ = Next year's expected dividend
• P₀ = Current stock price
• g = Dividend growth rate

**Example:**
Current stock price: $50
Next dividend: $2.50
Growth rate: 5%

Ke = ($2.50 / $50) + 5%
Ke = 5% + 5% = 10%

**Limitations:**
• Assumes constant growth forever
• Requires dividend-paying stock
• Sensitive to growth estimate

sections.title: Capital Structure Weights
sections.content:
**Preferred: Market value weights**

**Market Value of Debt:**
• Use market price of bonds
• Or book value if no market price

**Market Value of Equity:**
• Share price × Shares outstanding
• = Market capitalization

**Example:**
Market value of debt: $400 million
Market value of equity: $600 million
Total: $1,000 million

Weight of debt: 40%
Weight of equity: 60%

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: WACC Pitfalls
sections.content:
**Common mistakes to avoid:**

**1. Forgetting tax adjustment on debt**
• Always use AFTER-tax cost of debt
• Multiply by (1 - Tax Rate)

**2. Using book value weights**
• Prefer market value when available
• Book values can be outdated

**3. Wrong beta application**
• Beta adjusts equity cost, not debt cost

**4. Ignoring preferred stock**
• If present, add as third component
• Cost of preferred = Dividend / Price
• No tax adjustment (dividends not deductible)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: WACC = weighted average of after-tax debt and equity costs
After-tax cost of debt = Pre-tax rate × (1 - Tax rate)
CAPM: Ke = Rf + Beta × (Rm - Rf)
Higher beta = higher risk = higher required return
Dividend growth model: Ke = (D1/P0) + g
Use market value weights for capital structure
WACC is the discount rate for capital budgeting

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has: 30% debt (pre-tax cost 6%), 70% equity (cost 14%), tax rate 40%. What is WACC?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust debt cost for taxes before weighting.

title: Real Options in Capital Budgeting
description: Value managerial flexibility and strategic options in investment decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Traditional NPV assumes a fixed path - but managers can ADAPT! Real options analysis values the flexibility to expand, abandon, delay, or change projects as information unfolds. This is especially valuable for high-uncertainty investments.

sections.title: What Are Real Options?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Rights (not obligations) embedded in investment projects

**Financial options vs Real options:**
• Financial: Call/put on stocks (traded)
• Real: Options on physical assets/projects (not traded)

**Key insight:**
Standard DCF assumes now-or-never decision
Real options recognize you can WAIT, EXPAND, or ABANDON

**Value equation:**
Expanded NPV = Traditional NPV + Value of Flexibility

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: When Options Matter
sections.content:
**"VUCI" - Real Options Are Valuable When:**

**V**olatility is high (outcomes uncertain)
**U**ncertainty can be resolved over time
**C**ash flows are marginal (near breakeven)
**I**nvestment is somewhat reversible

**Low volatility + irreversible = Options don't add much value**
**High volatility + flexibility = Options are very valuable**

sections.title: Option to Defer Example
sections.content:
**Scenario:**
Project NPV today: -$2 million (would reject)

**But: Can wait 1 year to decide**
• 50% chance: Market improves, NPV = +$10M
• 50% chance: Market worsens, NPV = -$8M

**If invest today:**
NPV = -$2M (bad deal)

**If wait:**
• Good outcome: Invest, earn $10M
• Bad outcome: Don't invest, lose $0
• Expected value = 50%($10M) + 50%($0) = $5M
• Less delay cost (say, $0.5M) = $4.5M

**Value of waiting = $4.5M > -$2M!**
Option to defer is worth $6.5M

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Real Options Limitations
sections.content:
**When real options analysis is problematic:**

• **Hard to estimate volatility** for non-traded assets
• **Complex math** (Black-Scholes, binomial trees)
• **Can justify bad projects** with enough "option value"
• **Options may not exist** (competitors may move first)
• **Exercise not really optional** (sunk costs, contracts)

**Exam Approach:**
• Understand conceptually how options add value
• Recognize when flexibility is valuable
• Don't need to calculate option values mathematically

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Real options value managerial flexibility in projects
Types: defer, expand, abandon, contract, switch, stage
Expanded NPV = Traditional NPV + Option Value
Options most valuable with high uncertainty and flexibility
Option to defer: wait for information, avoid downside
Option to abandon: limit losses if project fails
Complements, doesn't replace, traditional NPV analysis

knowledgeCheck.question: A mining company buys mineral rights that allow (but don't require) mining operations to begin anytime in the next 10 years. This represents which type of real option?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: This is an option to defer. The company can wait to see how mineral prices evolve before committing to mining operations. If prices rise, they develop the mine. If prices stay low, they can wait or let the option expire.
knowledgeCheck.hint: What flexibility does buying the rights give them? The right to wait and decide later.

title: Economic Value Added (EVA)
description: Measure true economic profit by accounting for the cost of all capital

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Accounting profit hides the true cost of equity! EVA reveals whether a company is actually creating value for shareholders by charging for ALL capital - not just debt. Positive EVA means value creation; negative means value destruction.

sections.title: EVA Concept
sections.content:
**The Problem with Accounting Profit:**
Net income deducts interest expense (cost of debt)
But IGNORES the cost of equity capital
Shareholders expect returns too!

**EVA Solution:**
Charge for the cost of ALL capital employed

**EVA Formula:**
EVA = NOPAT - (Capital × Cost of Capital)

**Where:**
• NOPAT = Net Operating Profit After Tax
• Capital = Total invested capital (debt + equity)
• Cost of Capital = WACC

**Alternative:**
EVA = (ROIC - WACC) × Capital

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EVA vs Other Metrics
sections.content:
**Why EVA beats Net Income:**

**Net Income:** Can be positive while destroying value
(If returns < cost of equity)

**ROI/ROE:** Percentage only - ignores scale
(10% return on $100 < 8% return on $10,000)

**EVA:** Dollar amount of value created
(After charging for ALL capital)

**Both ROE and EVA have a place:**
• ROE for comparing efficiency across companies
• EVA for measuring absolute value creation

sections.title: EVA Calculation Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Operating income: $500,000
• Tax rate: 30%
• Total capital invested: $2,000,000
• WACC: 10%

**Step 1: Calculate NOPAT**
NOPAT = $500,000 × (1 - 0.30) = $350,000

**Step 2: Calculate Capital Charge**
Capital Charge = $2,000,000 × 10% = $200,000

**Step 3: Calculate EVA**
EVA = $350,000 - $200,000 = **$150,000**

**Interpretation:**
Company created $150,000 of shareholder value
Returns exceeded cost of capital by $150K

sections.title: Improving EVA
sections.content:
**Four Levers to Increase EVA:**

**1. Increase NOPAT (Same Capital)**
• Grow revenue
• Improve margins
• Operational efficiency

**2. Reduce Capital (Same NOPAT)**
• Better asset utilization
• Sell underperforming assets
• Reduce working capital

**3. Invest in Positive-EVA Projects**
• Projects with ROIC > WACC
• Grow capital IF returns exceed cost

**4. Reduce WACC**
• Optimize capital structure
• Refinance expensive debt
• Reduce risk (lowers cost)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EVA Adjustments
sections.content:
**Pure EVA requires adjustments to GAAP:**

**Common EVA Adjustments:**
• Capitalize and amortize R&D
• Capitalize operating leases (less relevant post ASC 842)
• Add back goodwill amortization
• Adjust for deferred taxes

**Purpose:** Make accounting numbers more "economic"

**CMA Exam:** Usually uses simplified version
Be aware adjustments exist but calculate with given data

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EVA = NOPAT - (Capital × WACC)
Positive EVA means true value creation (returns > all capital costs)
Net income ignores equity cost; EVA charges for ALL capital
Four levers: Increase NOPAT, Reduce capital, Invest wisely, Lower WACC
Alternative: EVA = (ROIC - WACC) × Capital
EVA gives dollar amount of value created/destroyed
ROI is ratio (efficiency); EVA is dollars (absolute value)

knowledgeCheck.question: Division A has NOPAT of $800,000 and invested capital of $5 million. Division B has NOPAT of $600,000 and invested capital of $3 million. If WACC is 12%, which division creates more economic value?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Division A EVA: $800,000 - ($5M × 12%) = $800,000 - $600,000 = $200,000. Division B EVA: $600,000 - ($3M × 12%) = $600,000 - $360,000 = $240,000. Division B creates more value despite lower NOPAT.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate EVA for each: NOPAT minus (Capital × WACC). Don't be fooled by absolute NOPAT.

title: Dividend Policy
description: Understand theories and factors affecting corporate dividend decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Should we pay dividends or reinvest? CMAs help analyze the trade-offs between returning cash to shareholders and funding growth. Understanding dividend policy theories helps communicate with investors and support board decisions.

sections.title: Dividend Policy Theories
sections.content:
**Dividend Irrelevance (Miller & Modigliani):**
• In perfect markets, dividends don't matter
• Investors can create "homemade dividends"
• Value depends on earnings, not payout

**Bird-in-Hand Theory:**
• Dividends are less risky than capital gains
• Investors prefer certain dividends
• Higher dividends → Higher valuation

**Tax Preference Theory:**
• Capital gains taxed at lower rate (often)
• Capital gains tax can be deferred
• Investors prefer lower dividends

**Signaling Theory:**
• Dividends signal management confidence
• Dividend increase = Positive signal
• Dividend cut = Negative signal

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Policies
sections.content:
**Types of dividend policies:**

**Stable Dividend:**
• Fixed amount per share
• Predictable for investors
• Reluctant to cut (signaling concern)

**Constant Payout Ratio:**
• Fixed % of earnings
• Dividends fluctuate with earnings
• Less predictable

**Residual Dividend:**
• Pay dividends only after funding investments
• Prioritizes positive NPV projects
• Most volatile

**Low Regular + Extra:**
• Small regular dividend
• Extra dividend in good years
• Flexibility without signaling concerns

sections.title: Factors Affecting Dividend Policy
sections.content:
**Internal Factors:**
• Liquidity position (need cash?)
• Investment opportunities (NPV projects?)
• Debt covenants (restrictions?)
• Access to capital markets
• Ownership preferences

**External Factors:**
• Tax laws (dividend vs capital gains rates)
• Legal restrictions (retained earnings limit)
• Inflation (real vs nominal dollars)
• Industry norms

**Life Cycle Impact:**
• Growth phase: Low/no dividends, reinvest
• Mature phase: Higher dividends, fewer opportunities
• Decline phase: High dividends, return capital

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Stock Dividend:**
• Additional shares to existing shareholders
• No cash leaves company
• Proportional ownership unchanged
• Accounting: Transfer RE to Common Stock

**Stock Split:**
• Increase shares, reduce par value
• 2-for-1: Twice as many shares at half price
• No dollar impact on equity
• Purpose: Lower share price, increase liquidity

**Reverse Split:**
• Decrease shares, increase price
• 1-for-5: One share for every five
• Purpose: Meet exchange requirements
• Often seen negatively

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cash vs Stock Dividends
sections.content:
**Key Distinctions:**

**Cash Dividend:**
• Reduces both assets (cash) and equity (RE)
• Creates tax liability for shareholders
• Company needs cash

**Stock Dividend:**
• No asset change
• Rearranges equity accounts only
• Generally not taxable to shareholders
• Company keeps cash

**Why issue stock dividend?**
• Conserve cash
• Lower share price (like split)
• Signal without cash commitment

**Neither changes shareholder wealth!**
(Just slices the same pie differently)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four theories: Irrelevance, Bird-in-Hand, Tax Preference, Signaling
Policies: Stable, Constant Payout, Residual, Low Regular + Extra
Factors: Liquidity, opportunities, covenants, taxes, life cycle
Stock dividends and splits don't change shareholder wealth
Growth companies retain; Mature companies pay dividends
Dividend cuts send negative signals (signaling theory)
CMAs analyze trade-offs between dividends and reinvestment

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the signaling theory of dividends, what does an unexpected dividend increase communicate to the market?
options: The company has no profitable investment opportunities
Management is confident about future earnings
The company has too much debt
Tax rates are about to change
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Signaling theory holds that dividend changes communicate management's private information about future prospects. An increase signals confidence in future earnings sustainability because managers are reluctant to cut dividends later.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Signaling theory focuses on what dividend changes communicate about management's view of the future.

title: Capital Structure Theory
description: Understand optimal capital structure and its impact on firm value

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How should we finance the company? The debt vs. equity decision affects cost of capital, risk, and firm value. CMAs analyze capital structure alternatives and their impact on WACC, EPS, and financial flexibility.

sections.title: Capital Structure Fundamentals
sections.content:
**Capital Structure:**
• Mix of debt and equity financing
• Expressed as D/E ratio or Debt/Total Capital

**Trade-off:**
• Debt is cheaper (interest is tax-deductible)
• But debt increases financial risk
• Goal: Find optimal balance

**Financial Leverage:**
• Using debt to magnify returns
• Good times: Leverage amplifies gains
• Bad times: Leverage amplifies losses
• More debt = More leverage = More risk

sections.title: Modigliani-Miller Propositions
rows: Cost of equity increases with leverage
Same, but offset partially by tax benefit
WACC constant at all debt levels

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Trade-Off Theory
sections.content:
**Balancing Benefits vs. Costs of Debt:**

**Benefits of Debt:**
• Tax shield (Interest × Tax Rate)
• Discipline on management (must make payments)
• Lower cost than equity

**Costs of Debt:**
• Financial distress costs
• Bankruptcy risk
• Agency costs (bondholder conflicts)
• Loss of flexibility

**Optimal Structure:**
Where marginal benefit of tax shield
equals marginal cost of distress

**Picture:** Upside-down U curve for firm value

sections.title: Financial Distress Costs
sections.content:
**Direct Costs:**
• Legal and administrative fees
• Court costs
• Accountant/advisor fees
• Typically 3-6% of firm value

**Indirect Costs (Often Larger):**
• Lost sales (customers worried)
• Lost suppliers (demand cash)
• Lost employees (leave for stability)
• Management distraction
• Fire sale of assets
• Inability to invest

**Key Insight:**
Distress costs increase with probability of distress
More debt = Higher probability = Higher expected costs

sections.title: Pecking Order Theory
sections.content:
**Alternative View:**
Companies prefer financing in specific order:

**1. Internal Funds (First Choice)**
• Retained earnings
• No transaction costs
• No information asymmetry

**2. Debt (Second Choice)**
• Less information-sensitive than equity
• Fixed claim, simpler to value

**3. Equity (Last Resort)**
• Signaling problem
• Issuing equity signals overvaluation
• Stock price often drops on announcement

**Implication:**
No "optimal" target - Structure reflects history of financing needs

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EPS-EBIT Analysis
sections.content:
**Comparing financing alternatives at different EBIT levels:**

**Find Indifference Point:**
EBIT where EPS is same under debt vs equity

**Formula:**
(EBIT - I1)(1-T) / Shares1 = (EBIT - I2)(1-T) / Shares2

**Above indifference:**
Debt financing = Higher EPS

**Below indifference:**
Equity financing = Higher EPS

**Note:**
Higher EPS doesn't mean better!
Debt increases risk - higher EPS at higher risk

**Don't confuse EPS maximization with value maximization!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital structure: Mix of debt and equity financing
M&M without taxes: Structure doesn't matter
M&M with taxes: More debt = Higher value (tax shield)
Trade-off theory: Balance tax benefits vs distress costs
Pecking order: Internal funds > Debt > Equity
Distress costs limit debt benefits at high leverage
EPS-EBIT analysis: Find indifference point between alternatives

knowledgeCheck.question: According to trade-off theory, what happens to the optimal debt level for a company with very stable cash flows and mostly tangible assets?
options: Lower optimal debt - stability means no need for tax shield
Higher optimal debt - lower distress costs allow more leverage
No change - optimal debt is the same for all companies
Cannot be determined without knowing interest rates
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Stable cash flows reduce probability of distress, and tangible assets have higher liquidation values, reducing distress costs. This allows the firm to take on more debt before marginal distress costs equal marginal tax benefits.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Trade-off theory says optimal debt is where tax shield benefit equals distress cost. What affects distress cost?

title: Mergers and Acquisitions Analysis
description: Evaluate M&A opportunities using financial and strategic analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: M&A transforms companies overnight! CMAs play critical roles in M&A - valuing targets, analyzing synergies, conducting due diligence, and integrating financial systems. Understanding M&A fundamentals is essential for corporate finance careers.

sections.title: Types of Business Combinations
sections.content:
**Merger:**
• Two companies combine into one
• One entity survives (or new entity formed)
• Usually friendly, negotiated

**Acquisition:**
• One company buys another
• Target may or may not continue
• Can be friendly or hostile

**Types by Strategic Rationale:**
• **Horizontal:** Same industry (Competitors)
• **Vertical:** Supply chain integration
• **Conglomerate:** Unrelated businesses

**Payment Methods:**
• Cash
• Stock
• Debt
• Combination

sections.title: Synergy Types
rows: Selling acquirer products to target customers
Combined entity gets better credit terms
Profitable company acquires one with NOLs

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: M&A Valuation Methods
sections.content:
**"CMD" - Three valuation approaches:**

**C**omparables (Market approach)
• Comparable company analysis
• Use multiples: P/E, EV/EBITDA
• "What are similar companies worth?"

**M**arket price
• Current stock price (if public)
• Add premium for control
• Typical premium: 20-40%

**D**CF (Discounted Cash Flow)
• Present value of future cash flows
• Most theoretically sound
• Highly sensitive to assumptions

**Reality:** Use all three and triangulate!

sections.title: Key M&A Metrics
sections.content:
**Acquisition Premium:**
= (Price paid - Target stock price) / Target stock price
• Typically 20-40%
• Reflects control value + synergies

**Break-even Synergy:**
= Premium paid / Target value
• Minimum synergies needed to justify premium

**Accretion/Dilution:**
• Does deal increase or decrease acquirer EPS?
• Accretive = EPS increases
• Dilutive = EPS decreases

**Pro Forma Analysis:**
• Combined financial statements
• Shows post-merger impact
• With and without synergies

sections.title: Due Diligence Areas
sections.content:
**Financial Due Diligence:**
• Quality of earnings
• Working capital normalization
• Hidden liabilities
• Revenue sustainability

**Operational Due Diligence:**
• Key customer/supplier concentration
• Technology and systems
• Employee retention risk
• Integration complexity

**Legal Due Diligence:**
• Contracts and commitments
• Litigation exposure
• Regulatory compliance
• Intellectual property

**CMA Role:** Lead financial due diligence, validate synergy assumptions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Why M&A Fails
sections.content:
**Most M&A destroys value for acquirer!**

**Common Failure Causes:**
• Overpaying (bidding wars)
• Overestimating synergies
• Underestimating integration costs
• Cultural incompatibility
• Key employee departures

**Winner's Curse:**
The "winning" bidder often overpaid
Other bidders saw problems you missed

**Synergy Reality Check:**
• Revenue synergies rarely materialize fully
• Cost synergies more reliable
• Implementation takes longer than planned
• Hidden costs appear during integration

**CMA Role:** Be skeptical, stress-test assumptions!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Merger vs Acquisition: Combination vs purchase
Horizontal, Vertical, Conglomerate: Based on strategic fit
Synergies: Revenue, Cost, Financial, Tax
Valuation: Comparables, Market price + premium, DCF
Typical acquisition premium: 20-40% over market price
Accretion/Dilution: Does EPS increase or decrease?
Due diligence: Financial, Operational, Legal validation
Most M&A fails - overpayment and integration issues common

knowledgeCheck.question: A target company's stock trades at $40. The acquirer pays $52 per share. What is the acquisition premium?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Premium = ($52 - $40) / $40 = $12 / $40 = 30%. This premium reflects the value of control plus expected synergies. The acquirer must realize at least 30% value improvement to justify the price paid.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Premium = (Price Paid - Market Price) / Market Price

title: Working Capital Investment Policy
description: Balance liquidity and profitability in current asset management

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital ties up cash! CMAs help optimize the balance between holding enough current assets to operate smoothly and minimizing the cash tied up in inventory and receivables. The right policy depends on your industry and risk tolerance.

sections.title: Working Capital Components
sections.content:
**Current Assets:**
• Cash and equivalents
• Marketable securities
• Accounts receivable
• Inventory
• Prepaid expenses

**Current Liabilities:**
• Accounts payable
• Accrued expenses
• Short-term debt
• Current portion of long-term debt

**Net Working Capital:**
= Current Assets - Current Liabilities
• Positive = Cushion
• Negative = Aggressive financing

**Working Capital Investment:**
Level of CA you choose to maintain

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Policy Trade-offs
sections.content:
**Aggressive (Low CA):**
• Minimum inventory levels
• Tight credit terms (low A/R)
• Low cash balances
• Risk: Stockouts, liquidity crises
• Benefit: Less capital tied up, higher returns

**Conservative (High CA):**
• Large safety stock
• Generous credit terms
• Large cash buffers
• Risk: Redundant assets, lower returns
• Benefit: No stockouts, easy payment

**Match to business:**
• Predictable demand → Aggressive OK
• Volatile demand → Conservative safer

sections.title: Cash Conversion Cycle
sections.content:
**CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO**

**Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO):**
= (Inventory / COGS) × 365
• Days to sell inventory

**Days Sales Outstanding (DSO):**
= (A/R / Credit Sales) × 365
• Days to collect receivables

**Days Payables Outstanding (DPO):**
= (A/P / COGS) × 365
• Days to pay suppliers

**Lower CCC = Less cash tied up**
• Reduce DIO: Lean inventory
• Reduce DSO: Faster collection
• Increase DPO: Slow pay (within terms)

**Negative CCC:** Collect before paying! (Amazon)

sections.title: Financing Policies
sections.content:
**Aggressive Financing:**
• More short-term debt
• Lower interest cost
• Higher refinancing risk

**Conservative Financing:**
• More long-term debt/equity
• Higher interest cost
• Lower refinancing risk

**Matching Principle:**
• Finance permanent CA with long-term
• Finance seasonal CA with short-term
• Lowest overall cost with acceptable risk

**Permanent vs Temporary CA:**
• Permanent: Base level needed year-round
• Temporary: Seasonal peaks above base

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Return vs Liquidity
sections.content:
**More current assets = Less risk, LESS return!**

**Why lower returns?**
• Cash earns little interest
• Inventory has carrying costs
• A/R ties up capital
• Opportunity cost of tied-up funds

**But too few current assets:**
• Lost sales (stockouts)
• Lost customers (tight credit)
• Inability to pay bills
• Higher short-term borrowing costs

**Find the balance:**
• Model trade-offs
• Consider industry norms
• Match to demand variability
• Factor in supplier reliability

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Net Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities
Aggressive: Low CA, higher risk, higher return
Conservative: High CA, lower risk, lower return
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (lower is better)
Matching principle: Match asset life to financing term
Permanent CA financed long-term; temporary CA short-term
Trade-off: Liquidity vs profitability

knowledgeCheck.question: A company with stable, predictable demand wants to maximize returns. Which working capital investment policy should it adopt?
options: Policy doesn't matter if demand is stable
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With stable, predictable demand, an aggressive (low CA) policy makes sense. The company can safely carry minimal inventory and cash because stockout risk is low. This minimizes capital tied up in current assets, maximizing returns. Unpredictable demand would warrant a more conservative approach.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When uncertainty is low, what level of safety buffer is needed?

title: Lease vs Buy Decisions
description: Evaluate the financial impact of leasing versus purchasing assets

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Equipment and real estate decisions involve billions in corporate spend! CMAs analyze lease vs buy alternatives to minimize cost and match financing to asset life. After ASC 842, both options appear on the balance sheet - making the analysis even more important.

sections.title: Lease vs Buy Overview
sections.content:
**Buying:**
• Own the asset
• Large upfront payment
• Depreciation deduction
• Maintenance responsibility
• Residual value risk/benefit

**Leasing:**
• Use without owning
• Lower initial outlay
• Lease payment deduction
• Maintenance may be included
• Flexibility to upgrade

**Key Question:**
What is the present value cost of each option?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Lease vs Buy Analysis
sections.content:
**Compare NPV of cash flows:**

**Buy Cash Flows:**
• - Initial purchase price
• - Maintenance costs (after-tax)
• + Tax savings from depreciation
• + Salvage value at end

**Lease Cash Flows:**
• - Lease payments (after-tax)
• + Tax savings from lease deduction
• - Lost salvage value

**Discount Rate:**
• After-tax cost of debt
• (Lease is financing decision)

**Choose option with LOWER NPV cost**
(or HIGHER NPV of savings)

sections.title: Quantitative Analysis Example
sections.content:
**Equipment Cost: $100,000**
• 5-year life, straight-line depreciation
• Salvage value: $10,000
• Tax rate: 30%
• After-tax cost of debt: 5%

**Buy Analysis:**
• Purchase: ($100,000)
• Annual depreciation tax shield: $18,000 × 30% = $5,400
• PV of tax shields: $5,400 × 4.329 = $23,377
• PV of salvage: $10,000 × 0.784 = $7,840
• Net cost: $100,000 - $23,377 - $7,840 = $68,783

**Lease Analysis (compare to $68,783):**
• Annual lease: $22,000
• After-tax: $22,000 × (1 - 0.30) = $15,400
• PV: $15,400 × 4.329 = $66,667

**Decision: Lease is cheaper ($66,667 vs $68,783)**

sections.title: Qualitative Factors
sections.content:
**Favors Buying:**
• Long asset life needed
• Specialized equipment
• Strong residual value
• Customization required
• Low obsolescence risk

**Favors Leasing:**
• Technology changes rapidly
• Preserve capital
• Off-balance sheet (pre-ASC 842)
• Flexibility to exit
• Bundled services

**Other Considerations:**
• Restrictions in loan covenants
• Alternative minimum tax
• State/local property taxes
• insurance requirements

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ASC 842 Impact
sections.content:
**ASC 842 changed lease accounting!**

**Old Rules (ASC 840):**
• Operating leases off-balance sheet
• Created 'synthetic debt'
• Lease vs buy had B/S impact

**New Rules (ASC 842):**
• Most leases on balance sheet
• ROU asset and lease liability
• Less B/S difference between options

**Decision still matters:**
• Cash flow timing different
• Tax treatment different
• Risk allocation different
• Flexibility different

**Don't assume 'leasing to stay off B/S' is still valid!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Compare NPV of after-tax cash flows
Buy: Purchase, maintenance, tax shields, salvage
Lease: After-tax lease payments, lost salvage
Use after-tax cost of debt as discount rate
Finance lease: Like ownership; Operating: Pure rental
ASC 842: Most leases now on balance sheet
Consider qualitative factors: Obsolescence, flexibility, capital

knowledgeCheck.question: In a lease vs buy analysis, which discount rate should typically be used?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The after-tax cost of debt is used because lease vs buy is a financing decision, not an investment decision. Both options provide the same asset use, so the question is which financing method is cheaper. Since leasing is an alternative to debt financing, use the debt rate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Leasing is a financing alternative, not a project investment.

title: International Finance Considerations
description: Navigate financial management in multinational operations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Global operations add complexity but also opportunity! CMAs in multinational companies deal with currency risk, transfer pricing, tax optimization, and cross-border cash management. Understanding these issues is essential as companies operate across borders.

sections.title: Key International Finance Issues
sections.content:
**Currency Risk:**
• Transaction exposure - specific transactions
• Translation exposure - financial statement conversion
• Economic exposure - competitive position

**Political Risk:**
• Expropriation
• Currency controls
• Regulatory changes
• Political instability

**Tax Considerations:**
• Different tax rates
• Withholding taxes
• Transfer pricing rules
• Tax treaties

**Operational Issues:**
• Cultural differences
• Legal systems
• Accounting standards (IFRS vs GAAP)
• Infrastructure differences

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Arm's Length Principle
sections.content:
**The Golden Rule of Transfer Pricing:**

Price transactions between related parties as if they were unrelated (arm's length)

**Why It Matters:**
• Prevents profit shifting to low-tax jurisdictions
• Tax authorities enforce aggressively
• Penalties for non-compliance

**Example Problem:**
• Parent in US (Tax rate 25%)
• Sub in Ireland (Tax rate 12%)
• Incentive: Overprice goods TO Ireland
• Result: More profit in low-tax country

**Tax Authorities:**
• IRS, OECD guidelines
• Documentation requirements
• Country-by-country reporting
• Advance pricing agreements (APAs)

sections.title: Cash Repatriation
sections.content:
**Bringing Overseas Cash Home:**

**Methods:**
• Dividends - Most common, may have withholding tax
• Management fees - For services provided
• Royalties - For IP usage
• Intercompany loans - Repayment
• Transfer pricing adjustments

**Tax Considerations:**
• Withholding taxes on dividends
• US tax on repatriation (pre-2017)
• Tax treaties reduce withholding
• GILTI and other anti-deferral rules

**Cash Pooling:**
• Centralize cash management
• Reduce overall borrowing
• Better liquidity management
• Notional or physical pooling

sections.title: International Working Capital
sections.content:
**Challenges:**
• Different banking systems
• Currency conversions
• Payment term expectations
• Collection difficulty
• Capital movement restrictions

**Solutions:**
• Regional treasury centers
• In-house banking
• Netting - offset intercompany flows
• Leading and lagging - adjust payment timing
• Reinvoicing centers

**Documentation:**
• Letters of credit
• Documentary collections
• Trade credit insurance
• Factoring

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Transfer Pricing Objectives
sections.content:
**Transfer pricing serves MULTIPLE goals - they can conflict!**

**Tax Minimization:**
• Shift profits to low-tax locations
• Subject to tax authority challenge

**Performance Evaluation:**
• Fair measure of subsidiary profit
• Manager motivation

**Goal Congruence:**
• Company-wide optimization
• Prevent suboptimal decisions

**Customs Duties:**
• Lower price = lower import duty
• Opposite of tax incentive!

**Key Point:**
No single transfer price satisfies all objectives!
Compromises required

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: International finance adds: currency, political, tax risks
Transfer pricing: Price intercompany transactions at arm's length
Methods: Market price, cost plus, resale minus, TNMM, profit split
Arm's length: Price as if parties were unrelated
Repatriation: Dividends, fees, royalties, loan repayment
Cash pooling: Centralize multinational cash management
Transfer pricing objectives can conflict (tax vs evaluation)

knowledgeCheck.question: When a comparable uncontrolled transaction price is available, which transfer pricing method is generally preferred by tax authorities?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When a comparable market price exists, the comparable uncontrolled price (CUP) method is most direct and preferred. Other methods are used when no comparable external transaction exists. CUP best reflects arm's length pricing.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which method most directly reflects what unrelated parties would charge?

title: Dividend Policy
description: Understand dividend decisions and their impact on value

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Dividends return cash to shareholders - but at what cost? CMAs analyze dividend capacity, forecast cash flows, and evaluate the trade-off between dividends and reinvestment. Understanding dividend policy helps with financial planning and stakeholder communication.

sections.title: Dividend Policy Theories
sections.content:
**Dividend Irrelevance (Miller-Modigliani):**
• In perfect markets, dividend policy doesn't affect value
• Investors can create 'homemade' dividends
• Value comes from investment decisions

**Bird-in-Hand Theory:**
• Investors prefer certain dividends to uncertain capital gains
• Higher dividends = higher value
• Reduce risk premium

**Tax Preference Theory:**
• Capital gains often taxed lower
• Lower dividends = higher value
• Tax timing advantage

**Reality:**
Market imperfections make policy matter

sections.title: Dividend Policies
rows: Predictable; may cut if earnings drop

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Decisions
sections.content:
**Factors Affecting Dividend Policy:**

**Legal Constraints:**
• Capital impairment rules
• Bond covenants
• State laws

**Liquidity:**
• Cash availability
• Not just earnings

**Investment Needs:**
• Good projects available?
• Retain for growth

**Tax Considerations:**
• Shareholder preferences
• Corporate vs individual rates

**Signaling:**
• Dividend cuts = bad news signal
• Increases = confidence in future

**Clientele Effect:**
• Attract investors who prefer your policy

sections.title: Stock Repurchases
sections.content:
**What is a Buyback?**
Company purchases own shares in market

**Methods:**
• Open market repurchase
• Tender offer (premium)
• Dutch auction
• Direct negotiation

**Why Repurchase?**
• Return cash (alternative to dividend)
• Signal undervaluation
• Offset dilution from stock options
• Increase EPS
• Tax advantages

**Impact:**
• Fewer shares outstanding
• Higher EPS (if earnings unchanged)
• Higher stock price (usually)
• Treasury stock on balance sheet

sections.title: Dividends vs Repurchases
sections.content:
**Dividends:**
