• Regular, ongoing commitment
• Taxed as ordinary income
• All shareholders participate
• Clear signal

**Repurchases:**
• Flexible, one-time
• Capital gains tax (often lower)
• Participation is optional
• Can time around stock price

**Trend:**
Repurchases have grown relative to dividends

**CMA Analysis:**
• Cash flow projections
• Investment opportunities
• Shareholder preferences
• Tax implications

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Dividend Mechanics
sections.content:
**Know the key dates!**

**Declaration Date:**
• Board announces dividend
• Creates liability

**Record Date:**
• Shareholders of record get dividend

**Ex-Dividend Date:**
• 1-2 business days before record date
• Buy after this = no dividend
• Stock price drops by ~dividend amount

**Payment Date:**
• Cash actually paid

**Stock Dividend vs Stock Split:**
• Both increase shares, no cash
• Stock dividend: Transfer from RE to C/S
• Stock split: No journal entry, just par value change

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: M-M: Dividend policy irrelevant in perfect markets
Bird-in-hand: Investors prefer certain dividends
Tax preference: Capital gains taxed lower than dividends
Policies: Stable, constant payout, residual, hybrid
Factors: Legal, liquidity, investments, taxes, signaling
Repurchases: Flexible alternative with tax advantages
Ex-dividend date: Buy after = no dividend; price drops

knowledgeCheck.question: A company decides to pay dividends only after funding all positive NPV projects. This is an example of which dividend policy?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Residual dividend policy pays dividends from earnings remaining after all acceptable investment opportunities are funded. This prioritizes investment but results in fluctuating dividends based on project availability and earnings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which policy treats dividends as a 'residual' after investment?

title: Business Valuation Methods
description: Apply valuation approaches to value businesses and projects

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Business valuation is central to major corporate decisions! CMAs support M&A analysis, strategic planning, and value creation initiatives. Understanding valuation methods helps evaluate deals, challenge assumptions, and communicate with investment professionals.

sections.title: Valuation Approaches
sections.content:
**Three Main Approaches:**

**1. Income Approach:**
• Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)
• Based on future cash flows
• Most theoretically correct

**2. Market Approach:**
• Comparable company analysis
• Precedent transactions
• Based on market prices

**3. Asset Approach:**
• Net asset value
• Based on balance sheet
• Used for asset-heavy businesses

**Multiple Methods:**
Use multiple approaches, triangulate

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DCF Valuation
sections.content:
**DCF Framework:**

**Enterprise Value =**
PV of forecast period cash flows
+ PV of terminal value

**Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF):**
EBIT
× (1 - Tax rate)
+ Depreciation
- Capital expenditures
- Δ Working capital
= Unlevered free cash flow

**Discount at WACC**

**Terminal Value:**
• Gordon growth: FCF × (1+g) / (WACC - g)
• Exit multiple: EBITDA × Multiple

**Equity Value = Enterprise Value - Net Debt**

sections.title: Comparable Company Analysis
sections.content:
**Process:**
1. Identify comparable companies
2. Calculate trading multiples
3. Apply multiples to target

**Common Multiples:**
• EV/EBITDA - Most common
• EV/Revenue - Loss-making companies
• P/E - Net income
• P/B - Asset-heavy

**Selection Criteria:**
• Same industry
• Similar size
• Similar growth
• Similar margins
• Similar risk

**Adjust for differences** in growth, margins, capital structure

sections.title: Enterprise Value vs Equity Value
sections.content:
**Enterprise Value (EV):**
• Value of entire business
• Market cap + Debt - Cash
• Independent of capital structure
• Use with EBITDA, EBIT, Revenue

**Equity Value:**
• Value to shareholders only
• Market cap (shares × price)
• Use with Net income, Book value

**Conversion:**
Equity = Enterprise - Net Debt
Enterprise = Equity + Net Debt

**Net Debt = Total Debt - Cash**

Use EV multiples when comparing across capital structures

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: DCF Assumptions
sections.content:
**DCF is Highly Assumption-Sensitive!**

**Key Sensitivities:**
• Revenue growth rate
• Margin assumptions
• Terminal growth rate
• Discount rate (WACC)

**Terminal Value:**
• Often 60-80% of total value
• Small changes in g or WACC = big impact
• Always sanity check

**Common Errors:**
• Terminal growth > GDP growth (unrealistic)
• Inconsistent assumptions
• Ignoring working capital
• Using EBITDA instead of FCF

**Best Practice:**
Run sensitivity analysis on key assumptions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three approaches: Income (DCF), Market (comparables), Asset
DCF: PV of projected cash flows + terminal value
FCFF = EBIT(1-T) + D&A - CapEx - ΔWC
Terminal value: Gordon growth or exit multiple
Enterprise Value = Equity + Net Debt
Equity Value = Enterprise - Net Debt
Comparable multiples: EV/EBITDA, P/E, EV/Revenue

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has Enterprise Value of $500M, debt of $150M, and cash of $30M. What is the Equity Value?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Equity Value = Enterprise Value - Net Debt. Net Debt = Debt - Cash = $150M - $30M = $120M. Equity Value = $500M - $120M = $380M. This represents the value available to equity shareholders.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Equity Value = EV - (Debt - Cash)

title: Capital Structure Theory
description: Understand optimal capital structure decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How should a company be financed - debt or equity? CMAs support capital structure decisions that affect cost of capital, risk profile, and shareholder value. Understanding the trade-offs between debt and equity financing is essential for strategic financial management.

sections.title: Capital Structure Basics
sections.content:
**Capital Structure:**
Mix of debt and equity financing

**Debt Characteristics:**
• Fixed payments (interest)
• Tax deductible
• Contractual obligation
• Lower cost (less risk to lender)
• Increases financial risk

**Equity Characteristics:**
• No required payments
• Not tax deductible (dividends)
• Residual claim
• Higher cost (more risk to investor)
• More flexibility

**Goal:** Minimize WACC, maximize firm value

sections.title: Capital Structure Theories
rows: Balance tax benefits vs distress costs
Prefer internal, then debt, then equity

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Trade-off Theory
sections.content:
**Value of Levered Firm:**

VL = VU + PV(Tax Shield) - PV(Distress Costs)

**Benefits of Debt:**
• Tax shield (Interest × Tax rate)
• Lower cost than equity
• Discipline on management

**Costs of Debt:**
• Financial distress (bankruptcy risk)
• Agency costs (debt covenants)
• Loss of flexibility

**Optimal Point:**
Where marginal benefit = marginal cost
WACC is minimized
Firm value is maximized

**Visual:**
As debt increases, WACC initially falls (tax benefit)
Then rises (distress costs dominate)

sections.title: Factors Affecting Capital Structure
sections.content:
**Favoring More Debt:**
• High tax rate (bigger tax shield)
• Stable cash flows
• Tangible assets (collateral)
• Mature industry
• Low growth (less need for flexibility)

**Favoring More Equity:**
• Volatile cash flows
• High growth potential
• Intangible assets
• High business risk
• Financial flexibility needed
• Restrictive debt covenants

**Industry Norms:**
Companies often target industry average

sections.title: Financial Leverage Effects
sections.content:
**Leverage Magnifies Returns:**
• Good times: Higher ROE
• Bad times: Lower (or negative) ROE

**Example:**
• Company A: 0% debt
• Company B: 50% debt at 8% interest
• Both have $100 assets, 30% tax rate

**If EBIT = $20:**
• A: ROE = $14 / $100 = 14%
• B: Interest = $4, EBT = $16, NI = $11.2
      ROE = $11.2 / $50 = 22.4%

**If EBIT = $5:**
• A: ROE = $3.5 / $100 = 3.5%
• B: Interest = $4, EBT = $1, NI = $0.7
      ROE = $0.7 / $50 = 1.4%

Leverage increases volatility of ROE

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: WACC vs Firm Value
sections.content:
**Relationship:**
• Lower WACC = Higher firm value
• Firm value = Cash flows / WACC
• They move INVERSELY

**Optimal Capital Structure:**
• MINIMIZES WACC
• MAXIMIZES firm value

**Common Exam Question:**
'At what D/E ratio is firm value maximized?'
Answer: Where WACC is minimized

**M&M Assumptions (unrealistic):**
• No taxes
• No bankruptcy costs
• No agency costs
• Perfect markets

**Real world:**
Trade-off theory more applicable

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital structure: Mix of debt and equity financing
Debt: Tax deductible, lower cost, increases risk
Equity: No tax benefit, more flexible, higher cost
Trade-off: Balance tax shield vs distress costs
Optimal structure minimizes WACC, maximizes value
Leverage magnifies returns (both up and down)
Stable companies can handle more debt

knowledgeCheck.question: According to trade-off theory, the optimal capital structure is where:
options: Tax shield is maximized regardless of distress costs
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Trade-off theory suggests the optimal capital structure is where the marginal benefit of debt (tax shield) equals the marginal cost (financial distress). This point minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value - neither all debt nor all equity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Where is the balance between debt benefits and costs?

title: Share Repurchases and Equity Management
description: Understand stock buybacks and their effects

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Companies spend billions on stock buybacks! CMAs analyze whether repurchases create or destroy value. Understanding the mechanics and motivations helps evaluate this significant use of corporate cash.

sections.title: Stock Repurchase Basics
sections.content:
**What is a Repurchase?**
• Company buys back its own shares
• Reduces shares outstanding
• Cash returned to shareholders

**Methods:**
• **Open market:** Buy on exchange
• **Tender offer:** Fixed price offer to all
• **Direct negotiation:** Buy from specific holder

**Treasury Stock:**
• Repurchased shares not retired
• Held by company
• No voting rights or dividends
• Can be reissued later

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Reasons for Repurchases
sections.content:
**Value-Creating Reasons:**
• Stock is undervalued
• Signal confidence
• Tax-efficient cash return
• Offset dilution from options
• Adjust capital structure

**Potentially Negative Reasons:**
• No good investment opportunities
• Manipulate EPS
• Support stock price artificially
• Executive compensation tied to EPS

**Value Neutral:**
• If stock is fairly valued
• Same as dividend economically
• Just different mechanism

**Key Question:**
Is the stock actually undervalued?
Management often thinks so (but they're biased)

sections.title: EPS Impact Analysis
sections.content:
**Simple Example:**
• Net income: $100M
• Shares before: 50M
• EPS before: $2.00

• Repurchase 5M shares at $20
• Cost: $100M
• Shares after: 45M
• EPS after: $100M / 45M = $2.22
• EPS increase: 11%

**But Consider:**
• Cash is gone ($100M)
• Could have earned return on cash
• If cash earned 5%: Lost $5M income
• Adjusted: $95M / 45M = $2.11

**True Test:**
Does after-tax yield on cash < earnings yield?
If yes, repurchase increases value

sections.title: Accounting for Treasury Stock
sections.content:
**Cost Method (Most Common):**
Dr. Treasury Stock (at cost)
Cr. Cash

**Balance Sheet:**
• Treasury stock = Contra equity
• Reduces total equity
• Shown at cost

**Reissuance:**
• If sold above cost: Credit APIC
• If sold below cost: Debit APIC (to extent available) then Retained Earnings

**Retirement:**
• Removes treasury and original issuance
• Permanently reduces shares

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Repurchase Analysis
sections.content:
**Repurchases Don't Always Create Value!**

**Overpaying:**
• If price > intrinsic value
• Wealth transfers TO selling shareholders
• FROM remaining shareholders
• Destroys value

**Opportunity Cost:**
• Cash unavailable for investments
• May reduce growth
• Compare to NPV of alternatives

**Leverage Effect:**
• Repurchases increase D/E ratio
• Using cash reduces equity
• May affect credit rating

**EPS Manipulation:**
• Higher EPS doesn't mean more value
• If funded by debt, risk increases
• Must consider cost of repurchase

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Repurchase: Company buys back shares, reduces outstanding
Treasury stock: Repurchased shares held by company
Methods: Open market, tender offer, negotiated
Increases EPS by reducing denominator
Only creates value if stock is undervalued
Compare to dividends: More flexible, often tax-preferred
Consider opportunity cost of cash used

knowledgeCheck.question: A company repurchases 10% of its shares when the stock is overvalued. This action:
options: Transfers wealth from remaining to selling shareholders
Has no effect on shareholder value
Always increases EPS and therefore value
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Repurchasing overvalued stock transfers wealth FROM remaining shareholders TO the selling shareholders. The company overpays for the shares, using cash that belonged to all shareholders to benefit only the sellers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Who benefits when the company overpays?

title: Working Capital Metrics and Analysis
description: Measure and optimize working capital performance

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital is the lifeblood of operations! CMAs track working capital metrics to ensure liquidity, optimize cash flow, and identify improvement opportunities. Understanding these metrics is essential for operational finance.

sections.title: Working Capital Basics
sections.content:
**Working Capital:**
Current Assets - Current Liabilities

**Components:**
• Cash and marketable securities
• Accounts receivable
• Inventory
• Accounts payable (subtracts)
• Accrued expenses (subtracts)

**Goals:**
• Ensure liquidity
• Minimize tied-up capital
• Balance risk and return
• Optimize cash flow timing

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cash Conversion Cycle
sections.content:
**Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC):**

CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO

**Interpretation:**
• Days between cash outflow (pay suppliers) and cash inflow (collect from customers)
• Lower is better (less cash tied up)
• Can even be negative (incredible!)

**Example:**
DIO: 45 days (hold inventory)
DSO: 30 days (collect receivables)
DPO: 60 days (pay suppliers)

CCC = 45 + 30 - 60 = 15 days

**Negative CCC:**
Some retailers collect before paying
Customers pay cash, suppliers wait
Amazon famous for negative CCC

sections.title: Operating Cycle vs Cash Cycle
sections.content:
**Operating Cycle:**
DIO + DSO
• Time from buying inventory to collecting cash
• Ignores when you pay suppliers
• Pure operations view

**Cash Conversion Cycle:**
DIO + DSO - DPO
• NET time cash is tied up
• Accounts for supplier financing
• Cash flow view

**Relationship:**
CCC = Operating Cycle - DPO

**Visual Timeline:**
1. Buy inventory (Day 0)
2. Pay supplier (Day 60 = DPO)
3. Sell inventory (Day 45 from purchase)
4. Collect cash (Day 45 + 30 = Day 75)
5. Cash outlay period: Day 60 to Day 75 = 15 days

sections.title: Improving Working Capital
sections.content:
**Reduce DSO (Faster Collections):**
• Tighten credit policies
• Offer early payment discounts
• Faster invoicing
• Better collection practices

**Reduce DIO (Less Inventory):**
• JIT inventory systems
• Better demand forecasting
• Eliminate slow-moving items
• Vendor-managed inventory

**Increase DPO (Slower Payments):**
• Negotiate longer terms
• Pay on due date, not early
• Centralize payables
• Balance with supplier relationships

**Trade-offs:**
Don't sacrifice sales for faster collection
Don't create stockouts for less inventory
Don't damage supplier relationships

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Days Calculations
sections.content:
**Common Calculation Errors:**

**Use Correct Denominator:**
• DSO: Credit Sales (or total sales)
• DIO: COGS (not sales!)
• DPO: Purchases (calculate if needed)

**Purchases Formula:**
Ending Inventory - Beginning Inventory + COGS

**Use Averages:**
(Beginning + Ending) / 2
For balance sheet items

**Two Formulas - Same Answer:**
• Days = (Avg Balance / Flow) × 365
• Days = 365 / Turnover ratio

**Example:**
A/R Turnover = 12 times
DSO = 365 / 12 = 30.4 days

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Working capital = Current assets - Current liabilities
DSO: Days to collect; DIO: Days inventory held; DPO: Days to pay
Operating cycle = DIO + DSO
Cash conversion cycle = DIO + DSO - DPO
Lower CCC = Less cash tied up = Better
Improve by reducing DSO/DIO, increasing DPO
Balance working capital efficiency with operational needs

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has DSO of 40 days, DIO of 55 days, and DPO of 35 days. What is the cash conversion cycle?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cash Conversion Cycle = DIO + DSO - DPO = 55 + 40 - 35 = 60 days. This means the company has cash tied up in working capital for 60 days between paying suppliers and collecting from customers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (subtract payables because they delay cash outflow).

title: Dividend Policy and Payout Decisions
description: Analyze dividend theories and payout strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How much profit to distribute? CMAs provide the financial analysis behind dividend decisions, balancing shareholder expectations with reinvestment needs. Dividend policy signals management's confidence and affects share value.

sections.title: Dividend Basics
sections.content:
**What are Dividends?**
• Distribution of profits to shareholders
• Cash or stock
• Declared by board of directors
• Not legally required (discretionary)

**Key Dates:**
• **Declaration date:** Board announces
• **Record date:** Who gets dividend
• **Ex-dividend date:** Stock trades without dividend
• **Payment date:** Cash distributed

**Key Ratios:**
• Dividend yield = Annual dividend / Stock price
• Payout ratio = Dividends / Net income
• Retention ratio = 1 - Payout ratio

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Policies
sections.content:
**Policy Types:**

**Stable Dividend:**
• Consistent dollar amount
• Grow gradually over time
• Most common approach
• Reduces uncertainty

**Constant Payout:**
• Fixed percentage of earnings
• Dividends fluctuate with profits
• Less predictable
• Reflects company performance

**Residual:**
• Pay dividends from leftover earnings
• After all +NPV projects funded
• Most erratic
• Investment first, dividends second

**Memory:**
• Stable = Smoothing
• Constant = Percentage
• Residual = Remainder

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Stock Dividend:**
• Additional shares to existing shareholders
• Pro-rata distribution
• No cash leaves company
• Stock price adjusts down proportionally

**Stock Split:**
• Increase shares outstanding
• 2-for-1 split: Own 100 shares → 200 shares
• Price adjusts proportionally
• Makes shares more affordable

**Economic Effect:**
• Neither changes shareholder wealth
• 'Cutting pizza into more slices'
• Psychological benefits
• Improved liquidity (stock split)

**Reverse Split:**
• Opposite of split
• Reduces shares, increases price
• Often signals distress

sections.title: Factors Affecting Dividend Policy
sections.content:
**Internal Factors:**
• Profitability and cash flow
• Investment opportunities
• Liquidity position
• Debt covenants
• Management preferences

**External Factors:**
• Tax laws (dividend vs capital gains)
• Investor expectations
• Industry norms
• Economic conditions
• Regulatory requirements

**Practical Considerations:**
• Cutting dividends = Very negative signal
• Companies reluctant to cut
• Build in cushion before raising
• Special dividends for one-time cash

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Dividends vs Buybacks
sections.content:
**Two Ways to Return Cash:**

**Dividends:**
• Regular payment to all shareholders
• Creates taxable event
• Expected to continue
• Cutting is very negative signal

**Share Buybacks:**
• Company repurchases own shares
• Optional for shareholder to sell
• Often tax-advantaged
• More flexible, no commitment
• Increases EPS by reducing shares

**Why Companies Prefer Buybacks:**
• Flexibility
• Tax efficiency
• Signal undervaluation
• Can cancel without penalty

**Exam Tip:**
Understand trade-offs between the two

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Dividends: Discretionary distribution of profits
Theories: Irrelevance, Bird-in-Hand, Tax Preference, Signaling
Policies: Stable dollar, Constant payout, Residual
Stock dividends/splits don't change shareholder wealth
Cutting dividends sends strong negative signal
Payout ratio = Dividends / Net income
Buybacks offer flexibility; dividends offer commitment

knowledgeCheck.question: A company pays dividends only from earnings remaining after funding all positive-NPV investment projects. This policy is called:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Residual dividend policy pays dividends from 'residual' or leftover earnings after all profitable investment opportunities are funded. This prioritizes reinvestment but creates variable dividend payments.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which policy treats dividends as what's left over after investing?
